speaker: interviewer
text: We'll let you know. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It's OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- in Austria, so far as I know, there were concentration camps, but we did not meet-- meet any of them. It's possible that there were no concentration camps in Austria and they were all confined to-- perhaps to Germany and to Poland. But in any event, from Austria-- well, while we were in Austria, one of our problems was collecting the displaced people who-- who had been victims of the Holocaust, and sorting out those people that wanted to go east and those that wanted to go west. As I said, there were very, very few people that wanted to go back east and fall in the hands of the Russians. So one of our-- so our problem changed from a mission of-- of combat to a-- at this point-- and the war had ended now in-- on May the 9th. Our problem soon became one of collecting these people, as an army, and putting them in camps, and getting them prepared both physically and spiritually to be able to go back and-- and resume their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in the process of setting up camps in Austria, although as a military unit, all we did was provide means to do this. And people like UNRRA and other agencies came in to do the actual work. From Austria, my division went into Czechoslovakia. And-- we did not, at the time, meet any victims of the Holocaust in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we then left Czechoslovakia after, perhaps, two weeks. And my division, and my unit in particular, went to Fussen, Germany. And Fussen is a Landkreis, which is the equivalent of Los Angeles County, south of-- south of Munich-- south and probably a little bit west of-- of Munich. Now, when I got there, the 317th had a series of-- of camps that they were-- for whom they were responsible. We had prisoner of war camps. But in addition to that, we had displaced persons camps. And it was here that I was introduced to some of the psychological after effects of the-- the Holocaust. I had a total of five camps for which I was responsible. And-- and I had to keep guard and all that sort of stuff. And there were two-- two things that stood-- stood out in my memory about these camps. The first of which was that every morning, I made it a practice to visit the camps on my rounds and talk to the head man, and see if there were any problems, and all that sort of stuff. And invariably, every morning, there were 3, 4, 1/2 a dozen suicides. In-- in particular, I remember one that was in a camp that was right across the street from my headquarters, in a-- in a German Kaserne. And this man, who I was later told was a Polish Jew, had taken his belt and wrapped it around his neck, and put the buckle on the post of a bed. And he was in a three-tier bed. And he had jumped off the bed. And he had strangled himself with-- this was like a very slow, painful death, because the belt had just simply gone around his neck very loosely. And then he had tied it. And then it-- it hung from the end. And it must have been a horrible death. I remember seeing him and his tongue hanging out. And it was really quite bad. But he was the only one. They-- you know, they-- they tried all sorts of ways-- they-- slashing their wrists, hanging themselves. Very few of them ever tried shooting themselves, because they had no weapons, obviously, because we had control of them. So that was the first problem. Now, the second problem that we ran into was just the human problem of trying to give these people some semblance of life again, because the camps were incredibly crowded. And for some reason, which is readily understandable, these people wanted to have children. They just-- and-- and one of our problems was trying to provide some-- some sort of privacy. There was no way to do it other than with-- with curtains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next rash of problems that we faced was how do we deal with all of these pregnant women, and how to, you know. Fortunately, as an army, we weren't too responsible for that. But we would constantly be asked for assistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the third problem, was oddly enough, one that I could very well understand. And that was that we began to have a crime wave. And the crime wave was very, very limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was happening was that at night, Germans in isolated farms and various areas were being killed. There was only one-- one explanation for this. And that was that the displaced people were simply exacting revenge, which was an understandable thing. And there was absolutely no way to-- no way to control it. I'm not sure at the time we cared, you know? Because we had other things to worry about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, the next problem that we ran into was we had some of these people that were Russians. And the Russians were demanding that we return all their nationals. And our government, for whatever reason, had said, yes, we can. And so what we found were Russian squads were coming in and trying to grab these people, which led to confrontations between us and the Russians. And in particular, these things would come to our notice. Because many of these people would come out knowing that the Russians were coming. They would come out, and they would slash their wrists or do something very dramatic to try to bring our attention to the fact that the Russians were doing this. This problem was-- was ultimately resolved when I think our government said to the Russians, look. You can't-- you can't just come down and try to take your nationals back. This is our territory. And if you do, you know, we're going to do things. So that-- that problem ultimately went away. Now, the next problem we had was we had lots of these displaced people now that had many skills. And they wanted to go to work, because they were now-- see this is-- this is August of-- of 1944. And the war had been over May, June, July, August. And many of these people had now been fed and-- and had been in hospitals. And so the next problem--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. And so the-- so the next problem was trying to find gainful occupations for-- for these people. So what we did was we created what were known as-- as the Polish guard units. Actually, they weren't just Poles. They, you know, they were-- they were all of these displaced people. And what they were were paramilitary units who then began to take over the defense of and-- and a-- there was no point about defense. Because there was no war. But it was the point of security to prevent the supplies from being stolen, and all that sort of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a large chunk of these people became members of these paramilitary units. In addition to that, many of them found work in-- in the officer's clubs and the enlisted men's clubs, as-- as waiters and-- and entertainers. And then ultimately, we gave-- we-- we were in a position to begin returning people home, in particular, the people that were to go to-- to France and those places.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oddly enough, we had people from Italy, which you-- you would suspect that-- that you wouldn't have. But we did. We had people from little principalities like Lichtenstein, you know, which is a postage stamp place, and Belgium, and Luxembourg, and France, and so forth. So gradually, that situation began to ameliorate itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, which was now December of 1945, I came home on a 45-day RR, which is Rest and Recuperation, and basically had no more to do with the situation. So now, would you like to ask questions? Sure. You told me that you then became an assistant to somebody and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, OK, yes. Thank you, Mary. When I came back from RR, I was assigned to Heidelberg, Germany. And I became the aide-de-camp to General Geoffrey Keyes, who was the commander of the army-- the US Army, OK? Now, there was a commander over him, but he commanded the troops. And part of my mission was to read everything that was submitted to him. Now at-- at this juncture, we had a very large force engaged in collecting Nazi documents and-- translating them, and then calling them for any information that was worthwhile. And it was at this point that I began to realize some of the other bestialities of this Holocaust thing that probably are very little known. But for instance, one day I ran across a document that was being submitted to the general in which in one of the camps-- and I've forgotten which one it was-- they were performing live vivisections on Holocaust victims. And the reason for this was that German airmen were being shot down over the Baltic Sea. And when they plunged into the water, the-- the water was so cold that they immediately went into some sort of a trauma. And if they were not rescued immediately, they simply lost the pilot, even though he may not have been wounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: So apparently, one of the things that they tried to do was to do some live vivisection experiments on the Holocaust victims to determine if there was any way to overcome the trauma of being immersed in the freezing water. And what they did was they would take the Holocaust victims, and they would plunge them into a vat of-- of freezing water. And then when the person lost consciousness, or even before, they would take them out. And in a state of hypothermia, they would then perform a vivisection on them without bothering to give them any anesthetics or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one of the experiments describing this as a-- a huge mass of yellow froth came out of this victim as they-- they opened them up. They tried other things like-- you know, they-- that obviously, the-- the purpose was to heat people up very quickly. And they tried other things. Like they would take one man out and then put him next to a woman. And then they would dutifully note in their notes that instant copulation took place in three or four attempts-- you know, this kind of weird science-- really weird. I elected not to-- if I remember correctly-- I elected not to pass that into the general, because I didn't see any reason why he had to read it. One of the other experiments that I read about was an elaborate attempt to determine the effect of poisons on the human body. And apparently, they did this very systematically. They would choose these people. And then they would give some of them poisons and not poisons. You know, it was a very elaborate, fake science kind of thing. And of course, they killed hundreds-- hundreds of people. There were other experiments that I don't recall, that no doubt are available in the literature. And this particular literature-- if I remember correctly-- became an issue about 10, 15 years ago. Because the medical profession-- some of-- some of the people in the medical profession said that they ought to read it. Other people said, morally, it was not good. I have no views on that except it seems to me if people died, and there was anything of value in there, their death should not have been in vain. So possibly, if it was anything in value, it should have been used. I never personally got engaged in the controversy, because it was purely medical. Now, one of the other things that I read about, and this struck me as being particularly horrible, was a-- a thing called a kinderblick. A kinder, as you know, is child. Blick means burn. And-- and this was a thing in which they described a concentration camp gala, you know, a festive occasion, in which a group of Nazi bigwigs and SS bigwigs were invited to this camp. And then they had this huge pit that was full of burning logs. And the SS men would then come in and pick up babies and young children and throw them into the burning pit. And they called it a kinderblicken-- a child burning. And this was entertainment for Nazi bigwigs. I think of all the things I read, this one struck me as the most ludicrous and hideous. Because if you've been around the Germans for any length of time, you know that these are a people that are very punctilious in rank and all this sort of stuff. And you can see these idiots saying, oh, no, here, general, after you, you know, and all this sort of stuff. And then they all sit down and watch babies being thrown in there. And they are all so polite and-- to each other in a stupid, almost illogical way. But anyway, that was one of the things that I did pass into the-- to general. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the documentation in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this has been translated. But there is documentation. And-- and somewhere it's available. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the documentation-- these documents you just describe, that you read, were in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the-- the-- the documents that I read were in English. And what they were were translations of documents that had been captured. The Nazis were inveterate file keepers. And so all of this is somewhere in the Nazi files. And if memory serves me correctly, some of the people that actually were in the camps have probably written about this. So there are probably books written by survivors that actually describe this, because it was a very common kind of thing. It's-- it's almost incredible, you know. It's unbelievable. But there it is. Now, the next-- the next problem that we ran into, at least while I was aide to the general, was there now became a-- there now grew a movement to begin to move the displaced persons out of Germany again. Because there were certain numbers of them that had no-- nowhere to go. They didn't want to go back to Russia. And they weren't from the east. They didn't have jobs. And so here-- here they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was at this point that all of the agitation began to move people to Israel. Now, I have to say that at this point, I believe that some of the-- what was it? Sin-- Shin Bet-- Shin Beth? There was a-- a movement in Israel that was-- Shin Bet-- I think that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these people were agitating to get the people moved to Israel. At the time, there was a lot of intergovernmental negotiations going on, because we, in effect, were caught in a-- in the middle of this thing. The British, of course, you know, were in control of Israel. We were sort of dedicated to the proposition that these people could go home. You had the ambiguity of the Balfour Declarations that had been made in which the Israelis had been promised a home-- or the Jews had been promised a home in Israel. And all of that was coming to a head. And at that point, I was transferred to command a-- what was known as constabulary problem-- constabulary squadron, which was in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --November of perhaps, 1945-- November, December. The 10th constabulary squadron was located in Kitzingen, Germany. And our next-- my next contact with the Holocaust victims was a relatively happy event for me, because my wife, who was in the Red Cross, was also very familiar with many of the people in the Lithuanian displaced persons camp that was located close by us. And I met my wife in November of 1945, and proposed, and was accepted. And-- and we were married in January of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my wife made arrangements with the ladies in the displaced persons camp to get costumes for her bridesmaid and her-- I guess, what is the first lady? It's not the best man. It's the best lady. Matron of honor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maid-- maid of honor. And these-- these costumes were lent to her for the wedding. In addition to that, as an aside, my wife had a parachute. And because there was no silk or anything else of that sort in Germany, her wedding dress was made out of the parachute that-- that she had. At that point, we came back to the United States. And my association with the Holocaust ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a-- a soldier that you met in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, OK. And I said I wouldn't tell his name. Well, in-- in the fighting in September of-- of 1944, which was a-- a very bloody-- very bloody battle. And in a sense, Murray and I are-- are survivors of a different kind of thing. We were fighting at a-- at little towns-- St. Genevieve and-- and Bazemont, OK. In October of 1944, I was approached by the inspector general and asked if I knew anything about a group of German prisoners that had been found in a barn at St. Genevieve. And I did not, because at the time, I had not been commanding the unit. I did not take command of the unit until after we had defeated the Germans at-- at St. Genevieve and had moved on. But it then occurred to me that one explanation of-- of why those Germans were there was that they had been attacking the G Company position-- the very unit that I had commanded-- and in which I told you was the soldier who had been a victim of the Holocaust himself in its initial stages. And one possible explanation for that-- it occurred to me years later-- was that this particular soldier had volunteered to take these people back and then had exacted his own revenge. There are other versions that, you know, that other people did it. So nobody will ever know the truth. My explanation is logical to me. And it also makes sense with respect to the actual events that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: That particular soldier was killed later in the war. So I do not choose to ever name him, although I know who he was. And-- and I would say that having been killed, he was-- he was glad that he had done his duty in the particular way he did. Because if what I think is the case is true, he certainly exacted a just revenge. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, would you like to introduce your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to introduce Murray Kaufman, who was one of the soldiers that served with me in the 317th Infantry. Murray was a volunteer. He did not get drafted. And he was one of the expert marksman in our regiment. He never missed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Murray was very badly wounded-- I guess it was in September, Murray?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: September-- and left the regiment prior to the time that we began having any contact with the Holocaust victims. Abe Lashin is a member of the 319th, which was another one of the regiments of the 80th Division. And he was badly wounded in the Battle of the Bulge, just before we began having contact. And you never returned to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: --division, I take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Murray never--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --returned. Because their wounds were so-- so severe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, are you-- can you show us some pictures now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I have some pictures. All of these pictures were-- were taken by people in-- in our unit. And most of them were very widely distributed in our regiment, beginning with the liberation of-- of Ohdruf. Many of my pictures that I had were destroyed in-- when my jeep was blown up. But I have some. And now I'd be very happy to show them to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me, sometime in 1944. And it's somewhere in France. At the time, I was a major. And I was the operations officer of the 317th Infantry. The operations officer is the man who does the-- the planning and conducts most of the details of-- of an attack or a defense. I had been promoted to major several months before. Shortly after this picture was taken, I was promoted to lieutenant colonel. And I had advanced through the ranks from 1942 to 19-- early 1945, from lieutenant to lieutenant colonel. And most of this was because the regiment had suffered so many casualties. And I had managed to stay alive. And simultaneously, I was able to do the job. Shortly after this, I was a-- a battalion commander. There are three battalions in a regiment. And during the course of the 10 months that we were in combat, the regiment lost 15 battalion commanders. This is a-- a map of-- of France, Germany, and Austria. And it shows the route of the 317th Infantry for the 10 months that it was in combat in the European theater of operations. The regiment landed, as you can see, at Omaha Beach, and engaged in the pursuit in the break out from the Normandy peninsula, and then ultimately ended the war in Austria. The salient points so far as the Holocaust is concerned, of course, was the City of Kassel, where I had described a personal liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Kassel is almost on the Rhine, but it's east of the Rhine. And it's on the Autobahn, north out of Frankfurt. It's perhaps 100 kilometers north of Frankfurt. The-- the next area was-- was Gotha, which was of importance. Because that's where we actually liberated Ohrdruf. And the next area was Weimar, where we liberated the Buchenwald. Jena was the city in which I described a great deal of activity. And then Gera, and subsequently a little bit east of that, was the area where I described the machine gunning of the--
